In [26]:
# At the other end of the extreme, we can fit separate (independent) means for each state. The only things that are shared in 
# this model are the coefficient for the Xs, and the standard deviation of the error.

import pystan                                                                                                                                        
import pandas as pd                                                                                  
import numpy as np 

code = """     

data {
  int<lower=0> N;               //the number of training observations    
  int<lower=0> K;               //the number of features/covariates    
  matrix[N,K] X;               //the model (features/covariates) matrix
  
  
  int y[N];                    //the response     
}


parameters {
  real alpha;                 //intercept
  vector[K] beta;            //the regression coefficients                                                        

} 

transformed parameters {                                                                             
  //vector[N] linpred;                                                                                 
  //linpred = alpha+X*beta;   // logit=P(y=1)=pi                                                                          
}


model {

   //--------------priors----------------------------
  alpha ~ cauchy(0,10);       //prior for the intercept following Gelman 2008                              
                                                                                                     
  for(i in 1:K)               // K=1, if one regressor                                                                                   
    beta[i] ~ student_t(1, 0, 0.03);                                                                 
    

 //------------likelihood-----------                                                 
  y ~ bernoulli_logit(alpha+X*beta);

}


"""
In [27]:
data = {
    'N':len(vacc),
    'K':9,
    'X':X2,
    'y':vacc,
}

print(data)

In [33]:
# At the other end of the extreme, we can fit separate (independent) means for each county. The only things that are shared in 
# this model are the coefficient for the basement measurement effect, and the standard deviation of the error.

varint_code = """     

data {
  int<lower=0> J;
  int<lower=0> N;               //the number of training observations    
  int<lower=0> K;               //the number of features/covariates  
  int<lower=1,upper=8> Z[N];    //group level predictor (regional codes)
  matrix[N,K] X;               //the model (features/covariates) matrix
  
  int y[N];                    //the response     
}


parameters {
  vector[J] alpha;
  //real alpha;                 //intercept
  vector[K] beta;            //the regression parameters                                                        

} 

transformed parameters {                                                                             
  vector[N] y_hat;                                                                                 
  //linpred = alpha[Z]+X*beta;   // logit=P(y=1)=pi  
  
  for (i in 1:N)
    y_hat[i] = alpha[Z[i]] + X[i] * beta;
}


model {

   //--------------priors----------------------------
  alpha ~ cauchy(0,10);       //prior for the intercept following Gelman 2008                              
                                                                                                     
  for(j in 1:K)               // K=1, if one regressor                                                                                   
    beta[j] ~ student_t(1, 0, 0.03);                                                                 
    

 //------------likelihood-----------                                                 
  //y ~ bernoulli_logit(alpha[Z]+X*beta);
  y ~ bernoulli_logit(y_hat);

}


"""
In [34]:
varint_data = {
    'J':len(pd.unique(Data1['REGCODE'])),
    'N':len(vacc),
    'K':9,
    'X':X2,
    'y':vacc,
    'Z':reg, # Stan counts starting at 1
}

print(varint_data)

bp = fit_var['alpha'].mean(axis=0)
mp = fit_var['beta'].mean()
for bi in bp:
    plt.plot(xvals, mp*xvals + bi, 'bo-', alpha=0.4)
plt.xlim(-0.1,1.1);
In [126]:
### Formulating a Varying Slope(s) Mixed Effects Model
In [42]:
#Varying Slopes Model......................

varslope_code = """     

data {
  int<lower=0> J;               //the number of regions
  int<lower=0> N;               //the number of observations    
  int<lower=0> K;               //the number of features/covariates  
  int<lower=1,upper=J> Z[N];    //group level predictor (regional codes)
  vector[N] X;                  //the model (features/covariates) matrix
  
  int y[N];                     //the response     
}


parameters {
  //vector[K] alpha;
  real alpha;                 //intercept
  vector[J] beta;            //the regression coefficients                                                        

} 

transformed parameters {                                                                             
  vector[N] y_hat;                                                                                 
  //linpred = alpha+X*beta[Z];   // logit=P(y=1)=pi  
  
  for (i in 1:N)
    y_hat[i] = alpha + X[i] * beta[Z[i]];
}


model {

   //--------------priors----------------------------
  alpha ~ cauchy(0,10);       //prior for the intercept following Gelman 2008                              
                                                                                                     
  for(j in 1:K)               // K=1, if one regressor                                                                                   
    beta[j] ~ student_t(1, 0, 0.03);                                                                 
    

 //------------likelihood-----------                                                 
  //y ~ bernoulli_logit(alpha+X*beta[Z]);
  y ~ bernoulli_logit(y_hat);

}


"""
In [43]:
varslope_data = {
    'J':len(pd.unique(Data1['REGCODE'])),
    'N':len(vacc),
    'K':1,
    'X':Data1['XE1'], #Based on Education Level 1 only
    'y':vacc,
    'Z':reg, # Stan counts starting at 1
}
In [44]:
SM_varslope = pystan.StanModel(model_code=varslope_code)

INFO:pystan:COMPILING THE C++ CODE FOR MODEL anon_model_f62a8013749fffe458ee2cd506c3c07f NOW.
